content: Hey Saturday, quick update — I started Andrew Ng’s ML Specialization and I’m on Course 2 now (advanced algorithms). Dr. Pradeep Krishnan at Lakeside University is mentoring me once a week. I want to make sure I’m learning the fundamentals the right way.

That’s a great setup—you’ve got both structured coursework and a mentor. Since you’re in Course 2, focus on really understanding cost functions, gradient descent behavior, and why neural nets outperform linear models on non-linear data. If you want, we can make a simple weekly study checklist you can share with Dr. Krishnan.

Yeah that’d help. I’ve been coding gradient descent from scratch in Python (no sklearn yet), and started a tiny neural net with NumPy. I get stuck on vectorization and shape mismatches all the time lol.

That’s super normal when implementing from scratch. A good habit is to print tensor shapes at each step and keep a small “shape contract” in comments (e.g., X: (m,n), W: (n,1)). Also test each function with tiny fake data before plugging into the full training loop.

Makes sense. At work I want to apply this to a real churn dataset from our SaaS product—about 48k customers, with features like login frequency, support tickets, plan type, and tenure. Target is churn in the next 30 days.

Perfect ML use case. Start with a clean baseline (logistic regression) and track AUC, precision/recall, and especially recall for churners since class imbalance is likely. Then compare against a small neural net and see if the lift is meaningful, not just statistically better.
Awesome. I meet Dr. Pradeep Krishnan next Tuesday (Mar 11), can you suggest what to show him so he can give good feedback fast?

Bring four things: your gradient descent + neural-net code, a one-page data dictionary, baseline metrics, and 2–3 specific questions where you’re blocked. Keep it to a 10-minute walkthrough so he can spend most of the time on critique. If you want, I can draft the exact agenda and question list now.
